main_summary.summary: Reverend Dr. Amos C. Brown, interviewed by David Cline in San Francisco on March 2, 2013, recounts his civil rights work from Jackson, Mississippi through the NAACP Youth Council network, Morehouse College, and Crozer Theological Seminary into his pastorate at Third Baptist Church San Francisco. Born February 20, 1941, Brown traces his lineage through Patrick Brown, a slave in Franklin County, Mississippi near the Forks of the Road slave market east of Natchez. He describes the 1955 murders of Reverend George Lee and Emmett Till, the Eddie Noel shooting in Holmes County, and Arenia Mallory's role at the Saints Industrial School in Lexington. Mentored by Medgar Evers beginning in 1955, Brown organized the first Mississippi NAACP Youth Council, traveled with Evers to the 1956 NAACP National Convention in San Francisco, was elected National Chairman of the NAACP Youth Work Committee in 1959, and was punished by Jackson school authorities including loss of his valedictorian honor at Jim Hill High School. He studied with Martin Luther King Jr. at Morehouse, joined the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights in Atlanta, and was arrested at the Livingston Park Zoo during the 1961 Freedom Rides. He has pastored Third Baptist Church San Francisco since 1976.
key_themes: NAACP Youth Council organizing in Mississippi
Civil rights and gay rights coalition advocacy
main_summary.historical_significance:
Brown's testimony documents the NAACP Youth Council pipeline that connected Mississippi, Oklahoma City, and Greensboro sit-in movements, including his claim that the student sit-down movement began with Clara Luper's Oklahoma City youth on August 28, 1958 rather than in Greensboro on February 1, 1960. He provides first-hand corroboration of the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission's surveillance of teenage civil rights organizers, naming bank vice president LATAM as a source of confidential financial information passed to Mayor Thompson and Jackson police.

As the last surviving member of the Atlanta Committee on Appeal for Human Rights that included Lonnie King, Charles Black, Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Marian Wright Edelman, Brown bridges the Mississippi NAACP youth network, the Morehouse-King mentorship, and the Crozer Theological Seminary pipeline shaped by J. Pius Barber. His subsequent advocacy linking civil rights to LGBTQ rights, refugee resettlement of Ethiopian and Eritrean refugees through Third Baptist, and Operation Crossroads Africa work extends the movement framework into the post-1965 era.

chapters.title: Introduction and Mississippi Birth
chapters.summary: David Cline opens the March 2, 2013 San Francisco interview with Reverend Dr. Amos C. Brown, who recounts being born in Jackson, Mississippi on February 20, 1941. He traces his great-great-grandfather Patrick Brown, born into slavery in Franklin County's Hamburg community east of Natchez.

chapters.title: The Forks of the Road Market
chapters.summary: Brown describes the area east of Natchez as a major dropping-off point for slaves from roughly 1820 to 1850, and recalls the 1830 cholera epidemic that quarantined incoming slaves at a site called Forks of the Road. He credits his father's sister with passing down this family history.

chapters.title: George Lee, Emmett Till, and Eddie Noel
chapters.summary: Brown notes his father succeeded Reverend George Lee, a Holmes County pastor murdered on Mother's Day 1955, the same year Emmett Till was killed. He then recounts the case of Eddie Noel, a 28-year-old World War II veteran and sharpshooter who shot a white man who had beaten him at a restaurant and evaded a posse of three to four hundred men for 18 days.

chapters.title: Arenia Mallory and the Saints School
chapters.summary: Brown describes Arenia Mallory, first principal of the Saints Industrial School in Lexington and a vice president of the National Council of Negro Women founded by Mary McLeod Bethune. He notes the school was sponsored by the Church of God in Christ, founded by Bishop Charles Harrison Mason after the Azusa Street Revival.
related_events: National Council of Negro Women founding

chapters.title: Eddie Noel's Surrender and Aftermath
chapters.summary: Brown recounts that Mallory convinced Eddie Noel to surrender after learning he had killed two deputies in addition to the first man. The judge ordered Noel examined at the Whitfield Mental Institution, he was never tried or convicted, and was later found to descend from Governor Edmund Noel, who served from 1909 to 1913.

chapters.title: Named for the Prophet Amos
chapters.summary: Brown recalls how the Eddie Noel and Emmett Till stories awakened him as a child of about eleven. He describes Miss Essie Collins, treasurer of the Jackson NAACP branch, telling him at age eight at Farish Street Baptist Church that his name meant the prophet who bears the burdens of the people.

chapters.title: Free by '63 and Sister Gloria
chapters.summary: Brown describes learning of a 1954 Dallas NAACP meeting his sister Gloria attended with Medgar Evers, where board chairman Channing H. Tobias declared the goal of being free by 1963. Gloria returned predicting an end to segregation and the right to vote, which excited Brown about the coming change.

chapters.title: First Youth Council and the Blue Goose
chapters.summary: Brown organized the first NAACP Youth Council in Mississippi in 1955, and in the summer of 1956 Medgar Evers asked his mother if he could travel to the San Francisco National Convention. They drove Evers's 1955 Oldsmobile, the Blue Goose, cooling the engine with ice bags across the desert.

chapters.title: First Hearing Martin Luther King
chapters.summary: Brown recalls first hearing Martin Luther King Jr. on youth night at the 1956 convention, shortly after the Montgomery victory. King gave a panoramic history and closed by saying he had a dream that all of God's children would be significant on the Constitution's keyboard, a speech Brown still possesses a copy of.

chapters.title: Third Baptist Church and Its History
chapters.summary: Brown recounts worshipping at Third Baptist Church during the 1956 trip and later returning as pastor to succeed Dr. Leon Howard Sullivan. He places its August 1, 1852 founding alongside the centennial of Caribbean abolition and the publication of Uncle Tom's Cabin, and credits Eliza Davis and her husband William with leaving white First Baptist over discrimination.

chapters.title: A Father Who Led by Example
chapters.summary: Brown explains that he had little direct conversation with his father, a busy country preacher who also worked as a janitor. Though aware of the movement and of discrimination, his father did not push him into activism, leaving Brown to find his own way through exposure to others.

chapters.title: Jackson Mentors and Movement Churches
chapters.summary: Brown names the mentors who shaped him: Medgar Evers, John W. Dixon, Essie Collins, Mary Cox who refused to ride segregated buses, Carsie Hall the letter carrier turned attorney, and youth council advisor Samuel Bailey. He identifies the few Jackson churches that opened their doors: Farish Street Baptist, Pearl Street AME, College Hill Baptist, and New Mount Zion Baptist.

chapters.title: The Sovereignty Commission File
chapters.summary: Brown describes the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission's ten-page file on him, opened in the 1980s, which reported that a vice president named LATAM at the Deposit Guaranty Bank broke confidentiality and told Mayor Thompson and the police chief about the bank account Brown had opened.

chapters.title: The Cleveland Plain Dealer Interview
chapters.summary: Brown recounts attending the 1958 NAACP convention in Cleveland with Medgar Evers and Joseph Broadwater, the year the Little Rock Nine were recognized. He gave an interview to the Cleveland Plain Dealer about Mississippi's double education system and unequal teacher pay, and someone forwarded the article to white superintendent Kirby P. Walker.

chapters.title: Barred from Jim Hill High School
chapters.summary: Brown describes being told by principal Luther Marshall in fall 1959 that orders from the white and Black superintendents barred him from re-enrolling at Jim Hill High School and to keep his mouth shut. Medgar Evers got with his mother and father and threatened a lawsuit over the deterioration of facilities, forcing the school to let him back in.

chapters.title: Denied the Valedictorian Honor
chapters.summary: Brown recounts that authorities abolished the student council he led to break his influence, though students elected him senior class president. He was valedictorian but was denied the honor and made salutatorian instead. Years later the principal broke down crying, explaining he had acted under pressure and threats to his job.

chapters.title: Spies and National Youth Chairman
chapters.summary: Brown details how Sovereignty Commission spies recorded license plates at mass meetings and got activists fired from their jobs the following week. He notes his continued NAACP work and his 1959 election as National Chairman of the Youth Work Committee in New York, where he was photographed with Roy Wilkins and Dr. King.

chapters.title: Clara Luper Reports from Oklahoma City
chapters.summary: Brown recalls presiding over the youth-unit reports at the 1959 New York meeting and receiving the report from Clara Luper, advisor of the Oklahoma City Youth Council, and Bob Rapose, its president. He marks August 28, 1958 as the first organized mass sit-down by young Black people under the NAACP umbrella.

chapters.title: The True Origin of the Sit-Ins
chapters.summary: Brown argues the student sit-in movement began in Oklahoma City on August 28, 1958, not Greensboro in 1960. He recounts that Ezell Blair Jr. carried the Oklahoma City model to A&T College and with three others sat down on February 1, 1960, after which the movement spread to Nashville, Tallahassee, Baton Rouge, and Atlanta. He credits National Youth Secretary Herbert Wright for laying the groundwork and notes the world press covered the story while American papers buried it.

chapters.title: Rosa Parks and the Birth of SCLC
chapters.summary: Brown emphasizes that Rosa Parks's NAACP training as Montgomery branch secretary and youth council advisor prepared her for her stand. He recounts meeting King in San Francisco in 1956, and explains that after Alabama outlawed the NAACP in 1956, King and others organized the Southern Christian Leadership Conference under the protection of the faith community.

chapters.title: Getting to Know Medgar Evers
chapters.summary: Brown describes Medgar Evers as a humble, warm, engaging man who became his surrogate father and elder brother, encouraging his talents and securing his travel to meetings. He recalls his mother never questioning Evers, and recounts the two of them disguising themselves as sharecroppers to hold mass meetings and investigate cases across the Mississippi Delta.

chapters.title: Fear and the Agitator Report
chapters.summary: Brown reflects that he was too young and daring to feel fear, though his life was truly in harm's way. He recounts the Sovereignty Commission's summation labeling him a full-fledged agitator to be kept active in their files, with records of his arrests in Atlanta and Savannah.

chapters.title: Desegregating First Baptist Atlanta
chapters.summary: Brown recounts desegregating First Baptist Church of Atlanta in 1961, which Dr. Benjamin Mays wrote about and which is now pastored by Reverend Charles Stanley. He met his wife, a Spelman student, there, after Morehouse and Georgia Tech students led a kneel-in and deacons physically threw them out of the church.

chapters.title: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights
chapters.summary: Brown describes being the youngest in the room when Atlanta college presidents urged the students to explain their cause publicly. With Lonnie King, Charles Black, Herschelle Sullivan, Julian Bond, and Marian Wright Edelman, and funding from Drs. Rufus Clement and Benjamin Mays, he helped author the Committee on Appeal for Human Rights in the Atlanta Journal Constitution, then sat in and went to jail with King, A.D. King, Otis Moss, and David Satcher.
keywords: Committee on Appeal for Human Rights

chapters.title: Rajmohan Gandhi and Moral Rearmament
chapters.summary: Brown describes meeting Rajmohan Gandhi, Mahatma Gandhi's grandson, in 1957 through Moral Rearmament, an organization founded by Frank Buchman in England and known earlier as the Oxford Movement. Brown found its emphasis on absolute honesty and purity somewhat naive, believing legislative action was also needed to change the legal underpinnings of discrimination.

chapters.title: Mackinac Island and Quaker Camp
chapters.summary: Brown recounts being invited to a world affairs gathering at Mackinac Island, Michigan, attended by representatives of some 87 countries, the same year King won the Spingarn Medal. In 1958 he spent a whole summer at a Quaker world affairs camp near Cornell University, broadening the horizons of a kid out of Mississippi.

chapters.title: Africa Scholarship and Crozer Admission
chapters.summary: Brown describes finishing Morehouse in 1964 and receiving a scholarship for his first trip to Africa, the same year he was admitted to Crozer Theological Seminary with a recommendation letter from Dr. King. He notes that King himself entered Crozer in 1948 at age 19, where his father was a close friend of Dr. J. Pius Barber.

chapters.title: J. Pius Barber and the Prophetic Tradition
chapters.summary: Brown profiles Dr. J. Pius Barber, editor of the National Baptist Voice and the second Black student to finish Crozer, who became the unofficial dean of its Black students. He names the prophetic-tradition preachers Barber mentored, including William Augustus Jones, Samuel D. Proctor, Harold Carter, and Charles Booth, describing a gospel that never bifurcated personal salvation from social justice.

chapters.title: Mordecai Wyatt Johnson at Howard
chapters.summary: Brown profiles Mordecai Wyatt Johnson, a Morehouse and Colgate Rochester graduate born in Paris, Tennessee, who pastored in Charleston, West Virginia before being elected Howard University's first Black president in 1926. He recounts Johnson's first agenda of elevating the night-school law school, asking Justice Louis Brandeis for advice on condition it stay secret until Brandeis died, and being told to teach young Black minds to master constitutional law.

chapters.title: Houston, Marshall, and Brown v. Board
chapters.summary: Brown describes Johnson recruiting Charles Hamilton Houston to build the law school, whose first class included Thurgood Marshall, Oliver Hill, and Robert Carter, with the mentorship of James Nabrit. He recounts the team arguing before the Supreme Court from 1953 on the 14th Amendment's equal protection clause, reversing Plessy v. Ferguson and breaking the legal barriers to discrimination.

chapters.title: The New Breed of Movement Ministers
chapters.summary: Brown observes that reports showed only about three percent of Black preachers were involved in the movement, calling it romanticized to say everyone was with Dr. King. He names the new-breed ministers who did join: C.K. Steel in Tallahassee, Father Gibson in Tampa, Benjamin Lawson Hooks in Tennessee, and Samuel Woodrow Williams who co-taught King's seminar at Morehouse.

chapters.title: King's Seminar on Personalism
chapters.summary: Brown describes the small eight-student social philosophy seminar he took with the down-to-earth Dr. King at Morehouse, where King taught the theory of personalism he acquired from Brightman at Boston University. The doctrine holds that every human being is endowed with dignity and worth that should be respected regardless of color or religion.

chapters.title: Women Preachers and Gay Rights
chapters.summary: Brown applies personalism to his embrace of women preachers, having ordained the city's first woman preacher at his church in 1984 despite most Black preachers opposing women in the pulpit. He extends the same logic to gay rights, arguing it is wrong to deny a marginalized people equal protection under the law.

chapters.title: John Mercer Langston in 1866
chapters.summary: Brown cites John Mercer Langston, the first Black congressman during Reconstruction from Virginia, who with Charles Satchel, the first pastor of Third Baptist, led Ohio's abolitionist movement before 1850. He keeps a copy of Langston's 1866 St. Louis speech demanding equal protection before the law and the right to vote.

chapters.title: Do Not Become What You Hate
chapters.summary: Brown tells fellow ministers they must not become that which they hate, urging that those who suffered discrimination for their skin color must not discriminate against gay, lesbian, transgender, and bisexual people. He observes pointedly that the divorce rate is highest not in liberal regions but across the Bible belt.
keywords: do not become what you hate

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: Religious Rites Versus Civil Rights
chapters.summary: Brown notes the King James who gave the Bible its English translation was gay, asking whether his contribution should be burned. He distinguishes religious rites from constitutional rights, invoking Roger Williams, run out of the Massachusetts Bay Colony in 1638, to argue that a free country is a democracy and not a theocracy where private doctrines are imposed on others.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: Refusing to Support Proposition 8
chapters.summary: Brown recalls a young man trying to take over his pulpit one Sunday morning when he announced he would not support Proposition 8, an act for which the man later apologized before the whole church. Brown believes he has been vindicated by the change that has come, including President Obama's position, having borne the burdens not just for Black people and women but also for gay people.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.summary: Brown frames human rights in a global picture, noting the Christian tradition holds that Christ died for the whole world, not just America. He warns that consuming a disproportionate share of the world's resources is unjust, and cites King's letter from the Birmingham jail to argue that injustice anywhere affects everyone everywhere.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: Leakey, the Rift Valley, and Kinfolk
chapters.summary: Brown invokes the paleontologist Leakey to argue that humanity's first home was the Rift Valley in Africa, so unkindness across races means messing with one's kinfolk. He recalls his mother's parting words to behave, lamenting that humanity grew arrogant and built an us-against-them dichotomy instead of recognizing one family sharing the world's resources.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: A Message to Young Men of Alpha
chapters.summary: Brown describes addressing students from Stanford, UC Berkeley, USF, and UCLA pledging Alpha Phi Alpha, the fraternity of King and W.E.B. Du Bois, urging them toward scholarship, deeds, and love for humankind. He holds up King as the model, recounting that King entered Morehouse at 15, was a C-plus student under hard taskmasters, then excelled at Crozer and earned his Boston University doctorate.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: Students of Promise and Education Crisis
chapters.summary: Brown describes the Students of Promise program at Martin Luther King Middle School, working with underachieving Latino and African-American males. He cites the grim statistics for Black students in the city, with mean averages around D-plus and only about 30 percent finishing high school, and urges these young men to reach back and pull others along as Mary McLeod Bethune taught.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: Origins of Operation Crossroads Africa
chapters.summary: Brown describes Operation Crossroads Africa, founded by Presbyterian pastor James Robinson of the Church of the Master in Harlem in 1958, the same year his Cleveland Plain Dealer article got him in trouble. The program engaged American and African students in summer study, travel, and community development, and gave John Kennedy the idea for the Peace Corps.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: The African-American Summit With Sullivan
chapters.summary: Brown recounts traveling to Liberia, Sierra Leone, Senegal, and Ghana on his first African trip and returning to the continent 22 times. He describes co-founding the African-American Summit with Dr. Leon Howard Sullivan, first held in Abidjan, Ivory Coast around 1991, born from a 1990 vision of pooling Black leaders, scholars, and business people to help Africa develop.

chapters.part: From Civil Rights to Human Rights
chapters.title: Resettling Ethiopian and Eritrean Refugees
chapters.summary: Brown describes the 1970s war in Ethiopia and Eritrea and the unfair U.S. quotas that admitted far fewer African refugees than Soviet, Cuban, or Southeast Asian ones. Through his congregation he sponsored about 3,000 refugees from Africa and the Caribbean, first taking them into his own family room before leasing a Victorian house near the church.

chapters.title: Discovering Kofi, the Ghanaian Prodigy
chapters.summary: Brown recounts attending Ghana's 50th independence anniversary and being moved by a 13-year-old keyboard prodigy who performed before Stevie Wonder. He arranged an 80 percent scholarship to Stuart Hall High School for Kofi, who went on to Morehouse and its conservatory, composing nine symphonies and two operas while planning a career in medicine.

chapters.title: Pass It On and Arriving at Morehouse
chapters.summary: Brown recalls Dr. Benjamin Mays telling him that the least one can do for undeserved blessings is pass it on to someone else. He describes arriving at Morehouse in 1959 with only $87 against an $895 room and board, supported by his home church, living with Dr. Mays, and working as chauffeur for Atlanta University president Rufus Clement.

chapters.title: Learning of Evers's Assassination
chapters.summary: Brown recounts how Morehouse classmates teased him about needing a visa to return to Mississippi, and how in the summer of 1963, while in summer school, a brother from Alabama told him a civil rights worker had been killed in Jackson. Brown immediately knew it was Medgar Evers, whom he had spoken with days before and who had sensed something would happen.

chapters.title: Freedom Rides and the Zoo Arrest
chapters.summary: Brown describes traveling alone to Jackson during the 1961 Freedom Rides and, on Evers's instruction, organizing protests after most riders were sent to Parchman prison. He was arrested at the Livingston Park Zoo over segregated benches, and arrested again that summer for insisting a young doctor not call his 85-year-old neighbor boy at the University of Mississippi Hospital.

chapters.title: The Evers Funeral and Police Violence
chapters.summary: Brown recounts being at Medgar Evers's funeral and the march afterward, walking three abreast behind the body before it was shipped to Washington for burial at Arlington. He describes police officers using billy clubs on children, women, and horses galloping over mourners, calling it a horrifying experience, and notes he stays in touch with Mrs. Evers and remains on the NAACP national board.

chapters.title: No Despair and Race Still Matters
chapters.summary: Brown says he has never had moments of despair, only greater determination to continue the fight Evers would not have wanted him to abandon. He argues race in America is like an untreated condition that will destroy the nation, citing the derailed Clinton race commission chaired by John Hope Franklin and warning that Obama's election did not make America post-racial.

chapters.title: Gun Violence and Anna Green
chapters.summary: Brown describes the daily climate of violence for African-American males between 15 and 30, leading funerals practically every month, and laments that the nation only got excited about guns after the Newtown shooting. He recalls Anna Green, the child killed in the 2011 Tucson shooting, and the clip of her at the piano singing Come Thou Almighty King.

chapters.title: Meeting with Vice President Biden
chapters.summary: Brown recounts a Friday meeting with Vice President Biden's chief of staff alongside Franklin Graham and Richard Land of the Southern Baptist Convention on gun violence. They agreed to convene faith leaders at the White House around the April 4 anniversary of King's assassination and endorsed universal background checks, expanded mental health programs, and addressing violent video games and movies.

chapters.title: America's Violent Culture and the Red States
chapters.summary: Brown contrasts the United States' 12,000 homicides a year with the dozens in other countries, calling America a violent culture that should check itself. He observes that the states demanding their guns are the old red states of the Confederacy, argues the agenda is driven by Republicans, and asks what boogeyman they are fighting in a nation meant to have liberty and justice for all.

chapters.title: The Beloved Community and the Salad Bowl
chapters.summary: Brown invokes John Winthrop's 1630 Model of Christian Charity sermon envisioning a city on the hill of mutual responsibility, urging Americans to turn to each other rather than against each other. He recasts King's beloved community as a salad bowl rather than a melting pot, where distinct cultures remain visible and the nation is healthier for its multicultural diversity.

chapters.title: Mississippi's Unsung Heroes and She-Roes
chapters.summary: Brown calls for resources to honor the movement's unsung heroes, naming C.C. Bryant and Doc Owens of McComb, Amzie Moore of Clarksdale, Samuel Bailey, Joseph Broadwater, Carsie Hall, and Reverend William Albert Bender of Tougaloo, who fought for equal teacher pay in 1946 and challenged the white primaries before Fannie Lou Hamer. He also names Gladys Bates, R.L.T. Smith, and the letter carriers who were independent of white employers.

chapters.title: States' Rights as a Coded Message
chapters.summary: Brown argues that segregationists' talk of states' rights and protecting against the tyranny of the government was a coded message against Black people, aimed at the federal government rather than state governments like Mississippi or South Carolina. He contends they invoked these arguments because they did not want equal protection of the law or justice for everybody.

chapters.title: The Second Amendment and Django
chapters.summary: Brown agrees with an argument, drawn from a Trudeau article citing the Constitutional Convention, that the Second Amendment protected the patrollers and southern militias that kept slaves suppressed. He references Andrew Delbanco's The Real American Dream on how weapons kept enslaved people from escaping, and the film Django, before the interview closes with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian sign-off.

loc_video.caption: Amos C. Brown oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in San Francisco, California, 2013 March 02
